items.name: Ep1 Scene 1: The Sky God's Price

sections.Narration:
Old NYAME sat upon his THRONE of cloud,
He kept all STORIES wrapped inside a shroud.
No tale was told, no LEGEND walked the earth,
The Sky God HOARDED every song of worth.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

From the scanty material we have at present, it would seem that the folk-lores of the coastal and hinterland peoples are substantially the same, the differences being traceable in many cases to the influence of the new environment. Thus, in the story where Anansi seeks to hide the wisdom of the world (Story II), which he has collected in a pot, among the coast peoples he finds a difficulty in climbing up the palm-tree, but among the forest tribes his difficulty is to get over the trunk of a tree which has fallen across the bush-path. Here the difference is due to environment and not to race.

It is necessary to point out that similarity of story cannot by itself be taken as indicative of similarity of race. Indeed, so common is it to find the same story told by people of varying types and in every stage of progress that it has opened up a problem of great importance. Have such stories originated from a common source? and, if so, where may the common origin be found? Or are folk-stories like the material productions of the races, i.e.

sections.Wonder: If you could buy all the stories in the world, what would you be willing to trade for them?
sections.Whisper: In Akan tradition, Nyame (also Nyankopon) is the supreme Sky God — omniscient but distant, like the Gnostic Pleroma. Anansi's bargain is a Promethean act: the small stealing FIRE (story) from the divine. The four impossible tasks mirror hero trials across cultures (Heracles, Psyche). Rattray (1930) records this as 'How It Came About That the Anansi Stories Were Known.' The Akan word for folk-tale is literally 'Anansesem' — Spider-stories.

sections.Wonder: What would the world feel like if nobody ever told a story? What would you miss most?
sections.Whisper: This is cosmological: the world-before-stories is the world before MEANING. In Yoruba philosophy, Odu Ifá teaches that the universe exists in potentiality until stories (ese) give it shape. The Akan concept of 'Sankofa' — go back and fetch it — suggests stories are not invented but RECOVERED. Compare Genesis: 'In the beginning was the Word.' Narrative is not decoration; it is the architecture of consciousness.

sections.Narration:

Whose body stretches all the river LONG.
Bring OSEBO, leopard fierce and PROUD,

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

This, from the utilitarian and Imperial standpoints, is as it should be, but it tends to be fatal to the mythology, the customs, and the traditions of such peoples as the negroes of West Africa. For this change is not taking place only in the direction of mere materialism. Christianity, entering the country through the ports, and Mohammedanism, being carried by Haussas along the trade routes from the interior, are playing their part in these psychological and sociological changes. The negro of yesterday differs from the negro of to-day as he in turn will differ from the negro of to-morrow. In view of all this metamorphosis it is much to be regretted that the geographical and linguistical difficulties have made the task of the folk-lorist not only difficult but wellnigh impossible. Much, of course, might be done if those whose duties carry them into the various districts would take in hand the task.

sections.Wonder: When someone tells you something is impossible, does that make you want to try harder or give up?
sections.Whisper: The four tasks encode the four ELEMENTS of West African cosmology: Onini (water/river), Osebo (earth/forest), Mmoboro (air/swarm), Mmoatia (spirit/unseen). Anansi must master all four realms to earn story-keeping. In Ifá divination, the 256 Odu (signs) similarly encompass all possible situations — total knowledge requires total encounter. Nyame's 'impossible' price is really an initiation: you cannot hold ALL stories unless you have faced ALL of nature.

sections.Narration:
ANANSI cut a BRANCH, both long and straight,

"My wife says you're NOT LONGER than this wood—
I bet she's wrong. Let's MEASURE if we could!"

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

Next day he took a long stick and set out in search of a boa-constrictor. When he arrived at the place where one lived he began speaking to himself again. “He will just be as long as this stick”—“No, he will not be so long as this”—“Yes, he will be as long as this.” These words he repeated several times, till the boa came out and asked him what was the matter. “Oh, we have been having a dispute in Nyankupon’s town about you. Nyankupon’s people say you are not as long as this stick. I say you are. Please let me measure you by it.” The boa innocently laid himself out straight, and Spider lost no time in tying him on to the stick from end to end. He then sent him to Nyankupon.

The third day he took a needle and thread and sewed up his eye. He then set out for a den where he knew a tiger lived. As he approached the place he began to shout and sing so loudly that the tiger came out to see what was the matter. “Can you not see?” said Spider. “My eye is sewn up and now I can see such wonderful things that I must sing about them.” “Sew up my eyes,” said the tiger, “then I too can see these surprising sights.” Spider immediately did so.

sections.Wonder: Anansi used the python's pride against him. Is it always wrong to be proud of what makes you special?
sections.Whisper: The python trick is PURE Akan wit: Anansi never uses force. He uses the opponent's own nature (pride, vanity, anger) as the trap. This is identical to Yoruba Eshu's method and to aikido's principle of redirecting energy. Rattray notes the Akan proverb: 'It is by SOFTNESS that you eat the snail.' The snake as first conquest echoes serpent symbolism across traditions — the kundalini must be mastered first.

sections.Whisper: The leopard in Akan cosmology represents EARTHLY power — chiefs, warriors, political authority. The Ashanti Golden Stool is protected by leopard-skin regalia. Intelligence governs strength. The pit-trap echoes: all power has a blind spot.

By and by, however, the other villagers heard these names and gradually every one got into the habit of calling the two friends by the nicknames in preference to the real ones. Finally, the King of the country heard of them and wished to see the men who had chosen such strange titles. He sent for them to Court, and they came together. He was much pleased with the one who had chosen the name of ‘Maku Fia,’ but he was annoyed at the other man’s choice and sought a chance of punishing him.

When he had talked to them a little while, he invited both to a great feast which he was to give in three days’ time. As they went away he gave a fine large yam to Maku Mawu and only a small round stone to his own favourite. The latter felt somewhat aggrieved at getting only a stone, while his friend got such a fine yam. Very soon he said, “Oh, dear! I do not think it is any use carrying this stone home. How I wish it were a yam! Then I could cook it for dinner.” Maku Mawu—being very generous—immediately replied, “Then change with me, for I am quite tired of carrying my great yam.” They exchanged, and each went off to his own home. Maku Fia cut up his yam and cooked it.

II. HOW WISDOM BECAME THE PROPERTY OF THE HUMAN RACE

There once lived, in Fanti-land, a man named Father Anansi. He possessed all the wisdom in the world. People came to him daily for advice and help.

One day the men of the country were unfortunate enough to offend Father Anansi, who immediately resolved to punish them. After much thought he decided that the severest penalty he could inflict would be to hide all his wisdom from them. He set to work at once to gather again all that he had already given. When he had succeeded, as he thought, in collecting it, he placed all in one great pot. This he carefully sealed, and determined to put it in a spot where no human being could reach it.

Now, Father Anansi had a son, whose name was Kweku Tsin. This boy began to suspect his father of some secret design, so he made up his mind to watch carefully. Next day he saw his father quietly slip out of the house, with his precious pot hung round his neck. Kweku Tsin followed. Father Anansi went through the forest till he had left the village far behind. Then, selecting the highest and most inaccessible-looking tree, he began to climb.

sections.Whisper: In Caribbean Anansi tales (Jamaica, Trinidad), Tiger replaces Leopard as the powerful one. This story migrated with enslaved Akan people and became a RESISTANCE parable: the small, enslaved person outsmarting the powerful master through wit. The 'riding' motif is deeply subversive — the powerless one literally MOUNTS power. In Jamaican Patois, 'Anansi story' still means a clever scheme. This is the diaspora's most beloved Anansi tale, encoded with the survival wisdom of the Middle Passage.

sections.Whisper: This fable encodes Akan metaphysics: the VISIBLE world (wiase) is governed by INVISIBLE forces (sunsum — spirit). In Yoruba thought, 'ashe' (the power to make things happen) is similarly invisible but more real than matter. The thread also prefigures string theory — the universe's fabric is woven from filaments too small to see.

sections.Whisper: This Igbo-origin tale (known widely as 'Anansi and Turtle') teaches RECIPROCITY — the fundamental law of West African ethics. The Akan concept of 'obra' (social responsibility) demands that guests be fed. Anansi's violation of hospitality law brings automatic CONSEQUENCE — not punishment from above, but natural balancing. In Yoruba ethics, 'iwa' (character) is destiny: how you treat others literally shapes your future. The turtle's underwater feast is the mirror-world where Anansi's rules don't apply.

sections.Whisper: This meta-tale explains the DIASPORA of narrative itself. Stories cannot be owned — they are commons, not property. The Akan concept of 'Anansesem' (spider-stories) as a GENRE means every folk tale is understood as part of Anansi's scattered web. When enslaved people carried these stories across the Atlantic, they fulfilled the prophecy: stories break every container. In information theory, this is the principle that information wants to be FREE. Anansi's failure to hoard stories is identical to his failure to hoard wisdom (the pot) — the same lesson at a higher octave.

— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

Near Anansi’s miserable little hut there was a fine palace where lived a very rich man called Nothing. Nothing and Anansi proposed, one day, to go to the neighbouring town to get some wives. Accordingly, they set off together.

Nothing, being a rich man, wore a very fine velvet cloth, while Anansi had a ragged cotton one. While they were on their way Anansi persuaded Nothing to change clothes for a little while, promising to give back the fine velvet before they reached the town. He delayed doing this, however, first on one pretext, then on another—till they arrived at their destination.

Anansi, being dressed in such a fine garment, found no difficulty in getting as many wives as he wished. Poor Nothing, with his ragged and miserable cloth, was treated with great contempt. At first he could not get even one wife. At last, however, a woman took pity on him and gave him her daughter. The poor girl was laughed at very heartily by Anansi’s wives for choosing such a beggar as Nothing appeared to be. She wisely took no notice of their scorn.

The party set off for home. When they reached the cross-roads leading to their respective houses the women were astonished.

sections.Whisper: Olodumare (also Olorun, Olofi) is the Supreme Being in Yoruba theology — distant, genderless, and beyond direct worship. Unlike the Abrahamic God, Olodumare does not intervene; the Orishas are the INTERMEDIARIES. This parallels Neoplatonic emanation: the One produces the Many. In Ifá, Olodumare is associated with the number 256 (the total Odu), representing COMPLETENESS. Verger (1957) emphasizes that Olodumare is not 'above' the Orishas hierarchically but is rather the SOURCE from which they differentiate — like white light splitting through a prism.

— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

This, from the utilitarian and Imperial standpoints, is as it should be, but it tends to be fatal to the mythology, the customs, and the traditions of such peoples as the negroes of West Africa. For this change is not taking place only in the direction of mere materialism. Christianity, entering the country through the ports, and Mohammedanism, being carried by Haussas along the trade routes from the interior, are playing their part in these psychological and sociological changes. The negro of yesterday differs from the negro of to-day as he in turn will differ from the negro of to-morrow. In view of all this metamorphosis it is much to be regretted that the geographical and linguistical difficulties have made the task of the folk-lorist not only difficult but wellnigh impossible. Much, of course, might be done if those whose duties carry them into the various districts would take in hand the task.

— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

From the scanty material we have at present, it would seem that the folk-lores of the coastal and hinterland peoples are substantially the same, the differences being traceable in many cases to the influence of the new environment. Thus, in the story where Anansi seeks to hide the wisdom of the world (Story II), which he has collected in a pot, among the coast peoples he finds a difficulty in climbing up the palm-tree, but among the forest tribes his difficulty is to get over the trunk of a tree which has fallen across the bush-path. Here the difference is due to environment and not to race.

It is necessary to point out that similarity of story cannot by itself be taken as indicative of similarity of race. Indeed, so common is it to find the same story told by people of varying types and in every stage of progress that it has opened up a problem of great importance. — W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

He went into the cottage and fetched a fine cooking-pot, which he presented to Anansi—telling him that he need never be hungry again. The pot would always supply enough food for himself and his family. Anansi was most grateful, and left Thunder with many thanks.

Being anxious to test the pot at once, Anansi only waited till he was again seated in the old boat to say, “Pot, pot, what you used to do for your master do now for me.” Immediately good food of all sorts appeared. Anansi ate a hearty meal, which he very much enjoyed.

On reaching land again, his first thought was to run home and give all his family a good meal from his wonderful pot. A selfish, greedy fear prevented him. “What if I should use up all the magic of the pot on them, and have nothing more left for myself! Better keep the pot a secret—then I can enjoy a meal when I want one.” So, his mind full of this thought, he hid the pot.

sections.Whisper: Shango is the HISTORICAL fourth Alaafin (king) of Oyo who was DEIFIED after death — one of the clearest examples of euhemerism in African religion. His double-headed axe (oshe) represents the balance of power and justice. In the diaspora, Shango becomes Changó (Cuba), Xangô (Brazil) — the most widely worshipped Orisha in the Americas. His sacred number is 6, his colors red and white. The thunder-stone (edun ara) falling from the sky links him to meteorite worship found across West Africa. Shango's story teaches that POWER without CHARACTER (iwa) self-destructs.

— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

Saying nothing to any one about the matter, he waited till morning. As soon as it was light he started off towards the shore, where the old boat lay. Getting into the boat, it started of its own accord and glided swiftly over the water—straight for the palm-tree. Arrived there, Anansi attached the boat as before and climbed the tree. This time, unlike the last, the nuts almost fell into his hands. When he aimed them at the boat they fell easily into it—not one, as before, dropping into the water. He deliberately took them and threw them overboard, immediately jumping after them. As before, he found himself in front of Thunder’s cottage, with Thunder waiting to hear his tale. This he told, the old man showing the same sympathy as he had previously done.

This time, however, he presented Anansi with a fine stick and bade him good-bye. Anansi could scarcely wait till he got into the boat—so anxious was he to try the magic properties of his new gift.

Her name comes from 'Yeye omo eja' — 'Mother whose children are fish.' In Yoruba cosmology, water is the PRIMAL element: life originates from the ocean. The story of her belly bursting to release rivers is a COSMOGONY — the universe born from the breaking of the maternal body. In Salvador da Bahia, February 2nd is Festa de Yemanjá — millions offer flowers to the sea. She is the most worshipped Orisha in Brazil, where she bridges African and Indigenous water spirits.

The elephants ate their way through the maize, but when they arrived at the other side they found that the boy had reached home. So they had to give up the chase and return to their village. The princess, however, refused to do so, saying, “I will return when I have punished this impudent fellow.”

She thereupon changed herself into a very beautiful maiden, and taking a calabash cymbal in her hand approached the village. All the people came out to admire this lovely girl.

She had it proclaimed through the village that whoever succeeded in shooting an arrow at the cymbal should have her for a bride. The young men all tried and failed. An old man standing by said, “If only Kwesi—the cutter of the princess elephant’s tail—were here, he could hit the cymbal.” “Then Kwesi is the man I will marry,” replied the maiden, “whether he hit the cymbal or not.”

Kwesi was quickly fetched from the field where he was ploughing, and told of his good luck. He, however, was not at all delighted to hear of it, as he suspected the maiden of some trick.

However, he came and shot an arrow which struck the centre of the cymbal. The damsel and he were accordingly married.

The pataki (sacred story) of the gods excluding Oshun is a FEMINIST cosmological text: the male Orishas' plans fail without the feminine principle. Oshun's mirror is not vanity — it is DIVINATION (she sees truth in reflection). In Ifá, Oshun is the only Orisha who learned the divination system, challenging the male monopoly on sacred knowledge. In Osogbo (Nigeria), her annual festival draws millions. In Cuba, she syncretized with La Virgen de la Caridad del Cobre — Cuba's patron saint.

sections.Whisper: Eshu (Elegba, Legba, Exú) is the most MISUNDERSTOOD Orisha — colonizers equated him with the Devil because he is the trickster-messenger. In truth, Eshu is the HERMES of Yoruba religion: divine messenger, guardian of crossroads, opener of communication. Without Eshu, NO ritual works — he must be propitiated first. In Ifá, Eshu is the 'enforcer' of sacrifice: if you promise something to the Orishas and don't deliver, Eshu scrambles your life. He is CHAOS in the mathematical sense — the sensitivity to initial conditions. Robert Pelton's 'The Trickster in West Africa' (1980) argues Eshu is the most philosophically sophisticated trickster figure in world mythology.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

Finding no way of escape from the difficulty, Ohia prepared for death. He first called all his friends and relatives to a great feast, and bade them farewell. Then he put his affairs in order—bequeathed all his gold to the faithful Awirehu, and his property to his son and servants. When he had finished, he went to the Assembly Place where the people of the neighbourhood were gathered together.

He first took leave of the chief, and then commenced his tale. He related the story of his many misfortunes—of his adventure with the deer, and of his promise to King Tiger. Finally, he explained the cause of his laughter which had annoyed his wife. In so speaking he fell dead, as the Tiger had warned him.

He was buried amid great mourning, for every one had liked and respected him. The jealous woman who had caused her husband’s death was seized and burnt as a witch. Her ashes were then scattered to the four winds of heaven, and it is owing to this unfortunate fact that jealousy and selfishness are so widespread through the world, where before they scarcely existed.

sections.Whisper: The griot (jeli in Mandinka, djeli in Bambara) is a HEREDITARY oral historian — born into the role, trained from childhood. The Malian proverb 'When a griot dies, a library burns' (attributed to Amadou Hampâté Bâ) captures the stakes. Griots memorized genealogies spanning 700+ years — the Kouyaté family can recite the Sundiata epic from 1235 CE. The kora (21-string harp-lute) is the griot's instrument, with a tuning system that encodes cosmological relationships. In the digital age, griot families now debate whether recording violates the oral COVENANT.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

The selection of tales has been carefully made, and in the retelling and illustrating of the story the object has been to give the reader an introduction to the thought and customs of the West African negro.

In order to produce the correct ‘atmosphere’ for the story, picture an evening scene in a native village. The sun is nearing the western horizon, seeming to fall like a huge ball behind the distant hills, the air is cool, and a solemn stillness prevails. Even the noisy youths and girls are quiet, and the time for tom-toms, crickets, bull-frogs, and the miscellaneous instruments of man and Nature for the production of the most weird and inharmonious of sounds is not yet. In the compound—the courtyard round which are the family dwellings—the women with their picin (children) on their backs are busy with mortar and pestle making foo-foo (native food from maize). Squatting near the mud walls, naked to the waist, their cloth forming but a covering for the loins, are a number of men smoking short clay pipes and expectorating in a most insanitary manner—a perfect picture of idleness.

sections.Whisper: The dundun (talking drum) exploits the TONAL nature of Yoruba — a language where pitch changes meaning (like Mandarin Chinese). The drum literally SPEAKS by mimicking speech tones. This is not metaphor; trained listeners can understand drum-speech at distances of 5+ miles. In 'The Drums of Africa' (Carrington, 1949), the talking drum network across the Congo basin is compared to early telegraph systems. The hourglass shape allows pitch-bending via arm pressure — an ANALOG synthesizer predating electricity by centuries. The dundun ensemble (iya ilu, omele) mirrors a conversational GRAMMAR: lead drum 'speaks,' supporting drums 'respond.'

Throughout the Gold Coast Colony and Southern Ashanti the stories as given by the various tribes are essentially the same. Actually, this does not appear to be so, though at present the data is not sufficient to enable one to form a definite opinion.

sections.Wonder: If you could design your OWN Adinkra symbol, what proverb or idea would it stand for?
sections.Whisper: Adinkra symbols are an IDEOGRAPHIC writing system — each of the 80+ symbols encodes a philosophical concept, proverb, or historical event. 'Sankofa' (go back and fetch it) has become the most famous globally, adopted by the African diaspora as a symbol of reclaiming heritage. The name 'Adinkra' comes from a defeated Gyaman king whose cloth was captured by the Ashanti. In semiotics, Adinkra functions like Egyptian hieroglyphs or Chinese characters — each symbol is simultaneously aesthetic, linguistic, and philosophical. The stamps are carved from calabash gourd and printed with 'adinkra aduro' (bark dye). UNESCO recognized Adinkra as significant cultural heritage.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

(The Bad Boy)

Once upon a time in a certain village lived a man and his wife who were childless. One day, however, when the husband was away hunting, the woman had a baby son. She was greatly troubled at her husband’s absence, because she was unable to let him know of the child’s arrival. In that country it is the custom for the father to give the baby its name when it is a week old. As the time approached for the naming, the woman wondered to herself what name she could give the child if her husband did not return in time. To her amazement, the child himself answered, “My name is Quarcoo Bah-boni.” As he was only a week old she was astonished to hear him talk. The next day she got a greater surprise. She had been grumbling because her husband was not there to go to the farm for her and fetch food. The baby announced, “I will go to the farm”—which he did.

When he was a few weeks old, she was one day very busy. She laid him down on the bed while she went on with her task. In a few minutes several boys came up to her in great anger. “Your son has been beating us and ill-treating us in the street,” said they. “My son!” she cried. “Why, my son is only a tiny baby.

sections.Whisper: The Akan day-name system (Kofi/Friday, Kwame/Saturday, etc.) is one of the most famous naming traditions in the world — Kofi Annan, Kwame Nkrumah. In Yoruba tradition, the naming ceremony (isomoloruko) on the eighth day includes SEVEN symbolic items: water (life flows), palm oil (smooth life), honey (sweetness), kola nut (happiness), salt (wisdom), pepper (tenacity), sugar cane (children). The practice of tasting bitter and sweet encodes the Yoruba concept of 'ori' — personal destiny — which includes both struggle and blessing. Names have PERFORMATIVE power: they are not labels but invocations.

Actually, this does not appear to be so, though at present the data is not sufficient to enable one to form a definite opinion.

The following is from a recent work on the Gold Coast, and presents a slightly different view from that in the quotation above: “The general sum of these traditions [regarding the origin of the tribes] is that the Fantis, Ashantis, Wassaws, and in fact all the Twi-[Tshi] speaking, or Akan, peoples, were originally one tribe.

sections.Whisper: The Sika Dwa Kofi (Golden Stool) is the most sacred object in Ashanti culture — believed to house the 'sunsum' (soul) of the ENTIRE nation. In 1900, the British governor demanded to sit on it, triggering the War of the Golden Stool led by Yaa Asantewaa (the queen mother). The British never captured it — the Ashanti hid it. The stool embodies a radical political philosophy: SOVEREIGNTY resides in the collective soul, not in any individual ruler. The king is 'occupant' of the stool, not its owner. Compare the Ark of the Covenant, the Japanese Imperial Regalia, the US Constitution — objects that embody collective identity.

sections.Whisper: The baobab tree is the AXIS MUNDI of the African savanna — its upside-down appearance (roots seemingly in the sky) has generated myths across the continent. In Akan culture, elders (mpanyimfo) are not just old people but holders of 'ntoro' — inherited spiritual knowledge. The concept of 'Ubuntu' (I am because we are) begins with LISTENING: wisdom is relational, not individual. In Igbo tradition, the 'ndi ichie' (council of elders) governs by consensus, not authority. The elder under the tree is the original CLASSROOM — all philosophy, law, science, and art were transmitted in this format for millennia before writing.

By and by, however, the other villagers heard these names and gradually every one got into the habit of calling the two friends by the nicknames in preference to the real ones. Finally, the King of the country heard of them and wished to see the men who had chosen such strange titles. He sent for them to Court, and they came together. He was much pleased with the one who had chosen the name of ‘Maku Fia,’ but he was annoyed at the other man’s choice and sought a chance of punishing him.

When he had talked to them a little while, he invited both to a great feast which he was to give in three days’ time. As they went away he gave a fine large yam to Maku Mawu and only a small round stone to his own favourite. The latter felt somewhat aggrieved at getting only a stone, while his friend got such a fine yam. Very soon he said, “Oh, dear! I do not think it is any use carrying this stone home. How I wish it were a yam! Then I could cook it for dinner.” Maku Mawu—being very generous—immediately replied, “Then change with me, for I am quite tired of carrying my great yam.” They exchanged, and each went off to his own home. Maku Fia cut up his yam and cooked it.

sections.Whisper: West African proverbs (owe in Yoruba, mme in Akan, ilu in Igbo) are not casual sayings — they are JURIDICAL tools used in court, negotiation, and conflict resolution. Chinua Achebe wrote: 'Proverbs are the palm oil with which words are eaten.' In Igbo law, cases were argued through competing proverbs. The Yoruba have an estimated 20,000+ proverbs, many encoded in Ifá divination verses. The 'it takes a village' proverb (attributed to various West African sources) became globally famous through Hillary Clinton's book, but its roots are in COMMUNAL child-rearing practices that are millennia old.

sections.Whisper: The Middle Passage (1500s-1800s) forcibly transported an estimated 12.5 million Africans to the Americas. This story does NOT minimize that horror — it honors the SURVIVAL mechanism. Enslaved people carried entire cosmologies in memory: Ifá divination, Anansi stories, medicinal knowledge, metallurgy, rice cultivation. Historian Michael Gomez ('Exchanging Our Country Marks,' 1998) documents how ethnic identities (Akan, Yoruba, Igbo) persisted and TRANSFORMED in the diaspora. The metaphor of stories-as-seeds is not decoration — it is literally how culture survived genocide.

He thought over the matter very carefully, but could not find a way of punishing Lizard. At last, however, he had an idea.

He went to the King and explained that he was setting off next morning on a long journey. He wished to start very early, and so begged the King’s help. The King had a fine cock, which always crowed at daybreak to waken the King if he wished to get up early. Anansi begged that the King would command the cock to crow next morning, that Anansi might be sure of getting off in time. This the King readily promised.

As soon as night fell Anansi went by a back way to the cock’s sleeping-place, seized the bird quickly, and killed it. He then carried it to Lizard’s house, where all were in bed. There he quietly cooked the cock, placed the feathers under Lizard’s bed, and put some of the flesh on a dish close to Lizard’s hand. The wicked Anansi then took some boiling water and poured it into poor Lizard’s mouth, thus making him dumb.

When morning came, Anansi went to the King and reproached him for not letting the cock crow.

sections.Whisper: Anansi's transformation across the diaspora is one of the best-documented cases of cultural SURVIVAL through story. In Jamaica, 'Anancy' stories were told at wakes (nine-night ceremonies) and became a vehicle for RESISTANCE — the trickster who outsmarts the powerful was clearly about enslaved people outwitting enslavers. In Curaçao, he became 'Nanzi'; in Suriname, 'Anansi'; in the US South, his stories merged with Cherokee trickster tales and became 'Aunt Nancy.' Emily Zobel Marshall's 'Anansi's Journey' (2012) traces this diaspora in detail. The transformation itself IS the lesson: identity is not fixed; it is a web that stretches without breaking.

sections.Whisper: The Br'er Rabbit tales, recorded by Joel Chandler Harris in 'Uncle Remus' (1881), are DIRECTLY descended from Akan Anansi stories via enslaved West Africans. Harris's framing (the kindly 'Uncle Remus' telling stories to a white child) is deeply PROBLEMATIC — it romanticizes slavery — but the stories themselves are African. The tar-baby story (Br'er Rabbit and the Tar-Baby) is the same as Anansi and the gum-doll. Florence Baer's 'Sources and Analogues of the Uncle Remus Tales' (1980) traces each story to specific West African originals. The briar patch trick is Anansi's METHOD: using the enemy's assumptions against them.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

“After an intercourse of some years with Europeans, the Tshi-speaking inhabitants of the towns and villages in the vicinity of the various forts added to their system of polytheism a new deity whom they termed Nana-Nyankupon—sometimes called simply Nyankupon. This was the god of the Christians, borrowed from them and adopted under a new designation. The great superiority manifested by the whites in their weapons, ships, manufactures—in short, in everything—convinced the natives with whom they had intercourse that they must necessarily be protected by a deity of greater power than any of those to which they themselves offered sacrifice, since their own deities had not, except very remotely, helped them to attain any such prosperity. They therefore gladly enrolled themselves amongst the followers of the god of the whites, and being informed that he dwelt in the heavens above, they denominated him Nana-Nyankupon, which may be freely translated ‘Lord of the Sky.’” [9]

The Gold Coast folk-stories are readily divisible into two groups, Anansi and non-Anansi tales. Anánsi is the spider, and with him is generally associated his son, Kweku Tsin (Tsĩ).

sections.Wonder: Call-and-response means one person sings and everyone answers back. Can you try it? Sing something, and have your family answer!
sections.Whisper: The ring shout is the MISSING LINK between West African circle dances and African American worship, jazz, gospel, blues, and hip-hop. Sterling Stuckey ('Slave Culture,' 1987) argues it is the foundational ceremony of African American culture. The counterclockwise circle movement mirrors Kongo cosmology's 'dikenga' — the cycle of life, death, and rebirth. Call-and-response (in Yoruba: 'owe — omo owe') is the fundamental GRAMMAR of West African music: one voice states, many voices affirm. This structure survived in gospel church (preacher/congregation), jazz (soloist/ensemble), and hip-hop (MC/crowd).

This, from the utilitarian and Imperial standpoints, is as it should be, but it tends to be fatal to the mythology, the customs, and the traditions of such peoples as the negroes of West Africa. For this change is not taking place only in the direction of mere materialism. Christianity, entering the country through the ports, and Mohammedanism, being carried by Haussas along the trade routes from the interior, are playing their part in these psychological and sociological changes. The negro of yesterday differs from the negro of to-day as he in turn will differ from the negro of to-morrow. In view of all this metamorphosis it is much to be regretted that the geographical and linguistical difficulties have made the task of the folk-lorist not only difficult but wellnigh impossible. Much, of course, might be done if those whose duties carry them into the various districts would take in hand the task.

sections.Whisper: The Gullah Geechee people of the Sea Islands (Georgia/South Carolina) preserved the most intact African cultural traditions in North America — due to geographic isolation on barrier islands. Their Creole language retains Mende, Vai, Kisi, and Yoruba vocabulary. Lorenzo Dow Turner's groundbreaking 'Africanisms in the Gullah Dialect' (1949) proved the African linguistic origins that scholars had denied. Gullah sweetgrass baskets are IDENTICAL to Senegambian coiled baskets — a craft tradition spanning 300+ years and 4,000 miles. In 2006, the Gullah Geechee Cultural Heritage Corridor was established by US Congress.

— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

The material in this book was collected in the following manner: The new educational policy of the Government provided for a Training Institution for Teachers at Accra. The first students to be admitted were men who had already had some considerable experience in the schools of their districts. They were, therefore, sufficiently familiar with the English language to express themselves clearly and fluently. At the same time they were men who could remember the time when the new civilizing forces at present at work were not nearly so pronounced. By obtaining from these students a variety of versions of the same story it became possible to some extent to eradicate the superfluous and the spurious.

The selection of tales has been carefully made, and in the retelling and illustrating of the story the object has been to give the reader an introduction to the thought and customs of the West African negro.

sections.Whisper: The Slave Codes of 1739 (following the Stono Rebellion in South Carolina) explicitly BANNED drums — one of the only instrument-specific laws in American history. The enslavers understood that West African drums were COMMUNICATION technology, not mere entertainment. The response was one of the great creative adaptations in human history: body percussion (hambone/patting juba), the banjo (derived from the Wolof 'xalam'), spirituals that encoded escape routes, and rhythmic preaching. Ned Sublette ('The World That Made New Orleans,' 2008) argues the drum ban CREATED American music by forcing rhythm into new containers.

The other animals all exclaimed in wonder when they saw the goat’s face, and asked him how it had happened. “I was unfortunate enough to get into a swarm of bees when I was working. They stung me,” answered the poor goat.

Next day it was the wolf’s turn to go to the farm. He also returned, much bruised and swollen. Goat (guessing what had happened) listened with a smile to the excuses made by Wolf to the others.

Goat and Wolf afterward talked the matter over and wondered much at the strength of the little boy.

Each day another animal took his turn at the farm, and each day he returned in the same condition as his friends had done. At last all the animals had been, and all now came together to discuss how best they might get rid of Quarcoo Bah-boni.

They made up their minds that, early the following morning, they would start off together and leave the boy in possession of the house. They prepared a big basket of food and set it ready.

Unfortunately for them, Quarcoo had heard their discussion and decided that he also would go with them.

sections.Whisper: Religious syncretism in the diaspora was not 'confusion' but STRATEGIC survival. Enslaved people mapped Orishas onto Catholic saints to practice in plain sight: Shango = Santa Barbara (both associated with lightning), Yemoja = Our Lady of Regla (both ocean mothers), Ogun = Saint Peter (both hold keys/iron). In Haiti, the Vodou revolution of 1791 — the only successful slave revolt in history — began with a ceremony led by Boukman, a Muslim from Jamaica. Brazilian Candomblé, Cuban Santería/Lucumí, Haitian Vodou, and Trinidadian Orisha are all LIVING religions with millions of practitioners today. They are not 'remnants' — they are thriving.

sections.Whisper: The concept of 'return' in the African diaspora is complex and contested. Pan-Africanism (DuBois, Garvey, Nkrumah) imagined literal return; Afrocentrism (Asante, 1980s) imagined intellectual return; genetic ancestry testing (21st century) offers molecular return. Saidiya Hartman's 'Lose Your Mother' (2007) complicates all of these — her trip to Ghana reveals that 'return' is impossible because the Africa that was left no longer exists. Yet the LONGING itself is generative: it produces culture, music, philosophy. Bayo Akomolafe would say the thread is not about going BACK but about noticing what is already HERE.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

IV. THUNDER AND ANANSI

There had been a long and severe famine in the land where Anansi lived. He had been quite unable to obtain food for his poor wife and family. One day, gazing desperately out to sea, he saw, rising from the midst of the water, a tiny island with a tall palm-tree upon it. He determined to reach this tree—if any means proved possible—and climb it, in the hope of finding a few nuts to reward him. How to get there was the difficulty.

This, however, solved itself when he reached the beach, for there lay the means to his hand, in the shape of an old broken boat. It certainly did not look very strong, but Anansi decided to try it.

His first six attempts were unsuccessful—a great wave dashed him back on the beach each time he tried to put off. He was persevering, however, and at the seventh trial was successful in getting away. He steered the battered old boat as best he could, and at length reached the palm-tree of his desire. Having tied the boat to the trunk of the tree—which grew almost straight out of the water—he climbed toward the nuts. Plucking all he could reach, he dropped them, one by one, down to the boat.

sections.Whisper: Kwanzaa was created in 1966 by Maulana Karenga during the Black Power movement — it is a MODERN invention drawing on ancient principles. The Nguzo Saba (seven principles) are synthesized from communal values across East AND West African traditions. The Swahili language was chosen as a pan-African lingua franca. Critics call it 'invented tradition'; defenders argue ALL traditions were invented — the question is whether the values are real. The kinara (candle holder) echoes the menorah, Advent candles, and Diwali lamps — a UNIVERSAL grammar of light-against-darkness. Kwanzaa is celebrated by an estimated 18 million people worldwide.

sections.Wonder: Hip-hop came from the same tradition as the griot! Can you make up a short rap about something you love?
sections.Whisper: The griot-to-MC pipeline is WELL-DOCUMENTED. Africa Bambaataa, hip-hop's 'godfather,' explicitly studied West African griots. The cypher (circle of MCs freestyling) mirrors the ring shout. DJing's 'call-and-response' between turntable and MC is identical to the dundun/vocalist relationship. Cheryl Keyes ('Rap Music and Street Consciousness,' 2002) traces specific flows to Wolof verbal dueling (tassou). The 'dozens' (ritual insult exchange) derives from West African 'song of derision' traditions. Even the word 'hip' may derive from the Wolof 'hipi' (to open one's eyes). Hip-hop is not influenced BY West African oral tradition — it IS West African oral tradition in new clothes.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

Accordingly, one day he went to Nyankupon and asked that, in future, all tales told by men might be Anansi stories, instead of Nyankupon stories. Nyankupon agreed, on one condition. He told Spider (or Anansi) that he must bring him three things: the first was a jar full of live bees, the second was a boa-constrictor, and the third a tiger. Spider gave his promise.

He took an earthen vessel and set out for a place where he knew were numbers of bees. When he came in sight of the bees he began saying to himself, “They will not be able to fill this jar”—“Yes, they will be able”—“No, they will not be able,” until the bees came up to him and said, “What are you talking about, Mr Anansi?” He thereupon explained to them that Nyankupon and he had had a great dispute. Nyankupon had said the bees could not fly into the jar—Anansi had said they could. The bees immediately declared that of course they could fly into the jar—which they at once did. As soon as they were safely inside, Anansi sealed up the jar and sent it off to Nyankupon.

sections.Whisper: Afrofuturism (coined by Mark Dery, 1993) is a cultural aesthetic that combines African mythology, science fiction, and technology. Sun Ra (jazz musician who claimed to be from Saturn) is considered its godfather. Octavia Butler's novels imagine African diasporic futures. Black Panther's Wakanda is peak Afrofuturism: vibranium technology + ancestral worship + pan-African design. Ytasha Womack ('Afrofuturism,' 2013) argues it is fundamentally about RECLAIMING the future from colonial narratives that imagined Africa as only 'past.' In Ifá divination, the Odu already contain EVERY possible future — Afrofuturism is just reading them with new eyes.

— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

The following is from a recent work on the Gold Coast, and presents a slightly different view from that in the quotation above: “The general sum of these traditions [regarding the origin of the tribes] is that the Fantis, Ashantis, Wassaws, and in fact all the Twi-[Tshi] speaking, or Akan, peoples, were originally one tribe. They were a pastoral race and inhabited the open country beyond the forest belt and farther north than Salaga. A northern and lighter-skinned people, which is commonly supposed to have been the Fulanis, commenced to encroach on their territory, and, being stronger than they, seized their cattle and young women and made many of the others slaves.... The subdivision of the united Akan race into its main branches, the Fantis and Ashantis, is variously accounted for.... One story very plausibly explains that the constant raids of their northern enemy, who burned all the farms, reduced the Akans to great straits for food.

sections.Whisper: Ubuntu (Nguni Bantu) literally translates as 'humanity toward others' but philosophically means 'I am because we are' — a complete rejection of Western individualism. Archbishop Desmond Tutu used Ubuntu as the philosophical basis for South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission (1996) — choosing RESTORATIVE justice over retributive justice. Michael Eze argues Ubuntu is 'the very human-ness that distinguishes us — the capacity to recognize the humanity in others.' In computer science, Ubuntu Linux was named for this concept. Bayo Akomolafe extends Ubuntu ecologically: not just 'I am because you are' but 'I am because the WORLD is' — including rivers, forests, spiders.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

“It can be observed that the people take a growing pride in the institutions and traditions of their country, that the chiefs are realizing the duties and influence of their position, and that public opinion, among the educated classes, at any rate, is beginning to recognize that some advance on the ideals and standards of the past must now be demanded.” [2]

This, from the utilitarian and Imperial standpoints, is as it should be, but it tends to be fatal to the mythology, the customs, and the traditions of such peoples as the negroes of West Africa. For this change is not taking place only in the direction of mere materialism. Christianity, entering the country through the ports, and Mohammedanism, being carried by Haussas along the trade routes from the interior, are playing their part in these psychological and sociological changes. The negro of yesterday differs from the negro of to-day as he in turn will differ from the negro of to-morrow. In view of all this metamorphosis it is much to be regretted that the geographical and linguistical difficulties have made the task of the folk-lorist not only difficult but wellnigh impossible.

sections.Wonder: The web was torn but it grew back even bigger. When something hard happened to YOU, did something good grow from it?
sections.Whisper: The 'rewoven web' is not romantic — it acknowledges the TEAR (slavery, colonialism, extraction) while honoring the GROWTH. Achille Mbembe ('Critique of Black Reason,' 2017) argues that the African diaspora produced a 'planetary consciousness' — a way of thinking about identity, belonging, and humanity that belongs to EVERYONE. The digital age has created new threads: Nollywood (Nigerian cinema, 3rd largest film industry globally), Afrobeats (Burna Boy, Wizkid — global #1 streams), African tech hubs (Lagos, Nairobi, Accra). The web is not 'returning' to what it was — it is becoming something that never existed before.

sections.SourceQuote:
— W. H. Barker & Cecilia Sinclair, West African Folk-Tales (1917) —

The following is from a recent work on the Gold Coast, and presents a slightly different view from that in the quotation above: “The general sum of these traditions [regarding the origin of the tribes] is that the Fantis, Ashantis, Wassaws, and in fact all the Twi-[Tshi] speaking, or Akan, peoples, were originally one tribe. They were a pastoral race and inhabited the open country beyond the forest belt and farther north than Salaga. A northern and lighter-skinned people, which is commonly supposed to have been the Fulanis, commenced to encroach on their territory, and, being stronger than they, seized their cattle and young women and made many of the others slaves.... The subdivision of the united Akan race into its main branches, the Fantis and Ashantis, is variously accounted for.... One story very plausibly explains that the constant raids of their northern enemy, who burned all the farms, reduced the Akans to great straits for food.

sections.Arc: Scenes: So here we are, at BEDTIME, you and me,... → He BOUGHT the tales, he TRICKED the migh... → So when you DREAM tonight, remember this...
sections.Wonder: You just heard 42 stories from one long thread. Which one will you dream about tonight?
sections.Whisper: This final story is META — it is Anansi AWARE of being in a story, looking at the reader/listener. In Akan tradition, every Anansesem ends with a formal CLOSING: 'This is my story, whether it be sweet or whether it be not sweet, take some and give some back.' The closing formula is itself an act of call-and-response: the story demands RECIPROCITY. You received these stories; now you owe the web a story of your own. Anansi doesn't just survive — he RECRUITS. Every person who tells a story becomes a spider in the web. Including you. Including this grammar. Including this very moment of reading.
